1: Ps 80: Asaph's Lament for the Northern Kingdom — 'Lilies of the Covenant.' The superscription 'Shoshannim Edut' (Lilies of the Covenant/Testimony) designates a tune also used in Ps 60. The Asaph psalm addresses YHWH specifically as the 'Shepherd of Israel' leading Joseph 'like a flock' — the northern tribal vocabulary (Joseph = Ephraim/Manasseh) suggests this psalm originates in or addresses the northern kingdom, possibly in the context of the Assyrian conquest of 722 BC. 'Give ear, O Shepherd of Israel' — ro'eh Yisrael (Shepherd of Israel) — the royal shepherd metaphor applied to YHWH: the divine king as the one who leads the covenant flock.
2: 'Before Ephraim and Benjamin and Manasseh, stir up your might and come to save us!' — the three tribes named are the Rachel tribes: Benjamin (Jacob and Rachel's youngest), Ephraim and Manasseh (Jacob's grandsons through Joseph and Rachel's descendant). These were the northern and central tribes most directly affected by Assyrian pressure. The ark-procession context may be implied: these tribes marched behind the ark in the wilderness (Numbers 2:18-24). The prayer 'stir up your might' (orerah et gevuratecha) is a summons for YHWH to awaken his dormant military power.
3: 'Restore us, O God; let your face shine, that we may be saved!' — the first occurrence of the three-part refrain that structures the psalm (vv3, 7, 19), with the divine title escalating: Elohim (God, v3) → Elohim Tzvaot (God of hosts, v7) → YHWH Elohim Tzvaot (LORD God of hosts, v19). 'Let your face shine' (haer panecha) — the Aaronic blessing language (Numbers 6:25) repurposed as national rescue plea. The shining face is the antithesis of the hiding face that has allowed the national disaster.
4: 'O LORD God of hosts, how long will you be angry with your people's prayers?' — the lament question 'how long?' (ad-matai) is the standard lament form (Psalms 6:3; Psalms 13:1-2; Psalms 35:17; Psalms 74:10; Psalms 79:5; Psalms 90:13). 'Angry with your people's prayers' — an extraordinary statement: YHWH is depicted as angry even at the prayers offered to him. The smoke metaphor (aneshan) in some readings: as smoke rises from an altar, so the prayers rise but YHWH responds with anger rather than grace. The prayer itself has become an occasion of divine displeasure.
5: 'You have fed them with the bread of tears and given them tears to drink in full measure' — 'bread of tears' (lechem dimah) and 'tears to drink in full measure' (bashalishet) — the covenant curse vocabulary: in the covenant blessings Israel would eat bread in plenty; in the curse, they eat grief. The measurement term (shalish, a third part or a large measure) indicates the quantity of grief is regulated — it is not random suffering but measured covenant discipline. Lamentations 3:15 similarly uses the 'bread of bitterness' image for the exile.
6: 'You make us an object of contention for our neighbors, and our enemies laugh among themselves' — the shame-dimension of the national disaster. 'Object of contention' (madon, strife) — Israel has become the source of mockery and dispute among neighboring nations. The enemies' laughter (yilagu) is the vindication of the enemy and the humiliation of YHWH's people: if YHWH cannot protect Israel, the nations conclude that YHWH himself has failed. The theological problem of national disaster is not only Israel's suffering but YHWH's reputation.
7: 'Restore us, O God of hosts; let your face shine, that we may be saved!' — second refrain, intensified to 'God of hosts' (Elohim Tzvaot). The divine title Tzvaot (hosts/armies) invokes YHWH's military capacity: the God of the heavenly armies is being petitioned by the earthly people who have lost their earthly battle. The refrain's repetition creates liturgical insistence: not satisfied by silence.
8: 'You brought a vine out of Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it' — the vine allegory (vv8-16) is the theological heart of the psalm. Israel as YHWH's vine is a sustained metaphor in OT prophecy (Isaiah 5:1-7: the Song of the Vineyard; Jeremiah 2:21; Ezek 15; 17; John 15). 'Brought out of Egypt' — the Exodus as the vine's transplanting: YHWH uprooted the vine from Egypt (slavery, Pharaoh's garden) and replanted it in Canaan. 'Drove out the nations' — the conquest as land-clearing for the vine.
9: 'You cleared the ground for it; it took deep root and filled the land' — the vineyard preparation: clearing (pinnita, clearing of rocks and underbrush), deep rooting (sheresh), and filling the land. The growth of Israel in Canaan under Joshua-to-Solomon is the vine's flourishing period. ANE vineyards required intensive preparation — the metaphor captures both divine labor and Israel's dependence on that labor.
10: 'The mountains were covered with its shade, the mighty cedars with its branches' — the vine's expansion at its height: covering the mountains and the cedars of Lebanon. This is the Davidic-Solomonic empire at its greatest extent: from the Sinai wilderness to the Euphrates (1 Kings 4:21). The cedars of Lebanon (Baal's territory in ANE mythology — cf. Psalms 29:5) are now shaded by Israel's vine — YHWH's people have overtaken even the domain of the rival gods.
11: 'It sent out its branches to the sea, and its shoots to the River' — 'the Sea' (yam) = the Mediterranean; 'the River' (nahar) = the Euphrates. The maximal extent of Davidic-Solomonic sovereignty. This is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic land promise (Genesis 15:18: 'from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates') and the Mosaic promise (Deuteronomy 11:24). The vine has filled the promised territory.
12: 'Why then have you broken down its walls, so that all who pass along the way pluck its fruit?' — the reversal: the vine that YHWH planted and protected (vv8-11) has had its walls broken down. The vineyard wall (geder) was the essential protection of an ANE vineyard — without it, animals and passersby could freely plunder. YHWH as the one who broke down the wall is the most disturbing image: the divine vinedresser has become the destroyer of his own vineyard. The same image in Isaiah 5:5 (YHWH breaking down the vineyard wall as judgment).
13: 'The boar from the forest ravages it, and all that move in the field feed on it' — the wild boar (chazir miyaar) from the forest is the ANE symbol of destructive, unclean, violent plunder. The pig was ritually unclean for Israel (Leviticus 11:7); its ravaging of the vineyard is doubly defiling — the unclean animal destroying the sacred vine. The Assyrian army (or any subsequent oppressor) functions as the boar: brutal, unclean, unstoppable. 'All that move in the field feed on it' — general pillaging following the major devastation.
14: 'Turn again, O God of hosts! Look down from heaven, and see; have regard for this vine' — the pivot to petition: shuv-na Elohim Tzvaot (return, please, O God of hosts). The 'return' (shuv) is the repentance/return vocabulary applied to YHWH: just as Israel must return (shuv) to YHWH, YHWH is asked to return his attention to the vine. 'Look down from heaven' — the divine descent from the heavenly viewpoint to see the damage. The vine now identified explicitly: 'have regard for this vine' (vephaqed gefen zot) — the paqad (visitation/oversight) of YHWH invoked for restoration rather than judgment.
15: '...the stock that your right hand planted, and for the son whom you made strong for yourself' — 'the son' (ben) — either the nation (Israel as YHWH's son: Exodus 4:22; Hosea 11:1) or the Davidic king (YHWH's son: Psalms 2:7; 2 Samuel 7:14). The ambiguity is productive: the vine-nation and its royal representative both claim the designation 'son.' YHWH's 'right hand' (yamin) — the planting hand (cf. v17: 'the man of your right hand').
16: 'They have burned it with fire; they have cut it down; may they perish at the rebuke of your face!' — the devastation described at its worst: fire and cutting down. ANE conquerors routinely destroyed vineyards and orchards as part of scorched-earth policy (Deuteronomy 20:19-20 restricts this for Israel's army). 'The rebuke of your face' (gaar panecha) — the same divine rebuke that drove the sea back (Psalms 104:7; cf. Psalms 76:6) invoked for the enemy's destruction.
17: 'But let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man whom you have made strong for yourself!' — 'the man of your right hand' (ish yeminecha) and 'the son of man' (ben adam) — the Davidic king as YHWH's appointed human representative. The phrase is unique: 'man of your right hand' positions the king as standing at YHWH's right hand (cf. Psalms 110:1). The 'son of man' designation, while here simply meaning 'human being,' feeds into the developing 'Son of Man' trajectory of Daniel 7:13 and the NT.
18: 'Then we shall not turn back from you; give us life, and we will call upon your name!' — the covenant vow: if YHWH restores, Israel will not again turn away (lo-nasog mimecha). The vow of not turning back is the anti-apostasy pledge — a recognition that the disaster arose from Israel's turning away. 'Give us life' (vechayyenu) — the divine life-giving that makes covenant loyalty possible: life is the prerequisite for calling on the divine name.
19: 'Restore us, O LORD God of hosts! Let your face shine, that we may be saved!' — the third and fullest refrain: YHWH Elohim Tzvaot — the complete divine title. The escalation from Elohim (v3) to Elohim Tzvaot (v7) to YHWH Elohim Tzvaot (v19) culminates in the covenant name. The final plea is grounded in the covenant name: YHWH — the God who revealed himself as 'I AM WHO I AM' at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14), who is the same God of the Exodus vine-transplanting — is asked to restore what his own name has promised.
